annotations.text: I shall therefore focus on neurogenic tumors of the posterior mediastinum as the primary neoplastic consideration in this infant.
Since they arise in the paravertebral sulci, the posterior margin is rarely outlined by air.
About 90 percent of patients with thoracic neural-crest tumors have elevated levels of dopa, dopamine, norepinephrine, normetanephrine, vanilmandelic acid (VMA), and homovanillic acid (HVA).
The presence of calcific densities within a posterior mediastinal lesion is highly suggestive of a neural-crest tumor.

the cleavage plane between the paraspinal tissues and the mass, the absence of spinal involvement, the absence of calcification, and the lack of evidence of distortion of adjacent structures such as the ribs, blood vessels, and airways make the diagnosis of a neural-crest tumor unlikely in this case from a radiographic standpoint.
As an ovoid mass in the paraspinal region a bronchogenic cyst may simulate a neurogenic tumor.
I would have obtained an assay for urinary catecholamine values and additional imaging to delineate the vascular features, and I would have proceeded with an exploratory thoracotomy.
I propose that a thoracotomy was performed and that an extralobar sequestration was detected.
The disorganized architecture in this case is typical of extralobar sequestrations and resembles that seen in one type of cystic adenomatoid malformation.
Extralobar sequestration and cystic adenomatoid malformation may coexist and may be related histogenetically to each other and to bronchogenic cysts.
lymphangiectasis presumably results from interruption of the normal egress of lymph along the hilar structures to the mediastinum.
Approximately 50 percent of patients with extralobar sequestration have a variety of other congenital anomalies, particularly diaphragmatic defects, but none have been demonstrated in this patient.
The indication for an operation was to eliminate the risk of recurrent respiratory problems and to rule out a tumor.
Sequestered lobes tend to become infected and may be complicated by abscess formation.
The observation of lymphangiectasia in this case suggests such a mechanism.
It is more likely that sequestered segments become contaminated through the airways and become infected because of impaired lymphatic drainage.
